steps.narrationText: Our working fork holds version one of the app state — a simple marker file makes it visible.
It is not just files. A real PostgreSQL database lives in this fork too, and the customers table holds three rows. Remember that number.
Freeze it. The commit captures the entire repository as an immutable image, with a message, an author, and a parent. Exactly like git, but for everything, database included.
Give the commit a name. The stable branch now points at version one.
Keep working. Version two replaces version one in the working fork. The commit does not care.
Now the disaster. The risky change drops the customers table. In real life this is the moment your stomach sinks.
Ask for the rows again and the database says it straight: relation customers does not exist. The disaster is real. Every row is gone.
Commit the risky change too, broken table and all. History records what actually happened. Two commits now, parent and child.
And there is the history: version two on top, version one below it, messages and authors included, without unlocking a single image.
Now the payoff. Check the stable branch out into a brand new writable fork. Reflink clone: near-instant, any size.
Start the rollback fork like any other repo.
And the proof: the rollback fork reads version one. The working fork still has version two. History you can run.
And the part that matters most: the dropped table is back, all three rows of it. The rollback restored the database, not just the files.

narrationAudio.narrationText: Let's version your repositories like code! We'll freeze a checkpoint, give it a name, then drop a real database table. The rollback brings back the exact frozen state, every row included, in seconds.

The model is git, but for whole repositories. A commit is a frozen, immutable image. It refuses to even mount, so nothing can ever change it. A branch is just a name pointing at a commit. And checkout turns any commit back into a fresh, writable fork.
Seeing is believing. The rollback fork on the left holds version one, while the working fork on the right moved on to version two. Both live, both writable, both from the same history.
Commits never change, forks never wait. Next up, we move a whole running repository to another machine, live. See you in the next one!
